confidence.disclosure: Nana Asma'u did not travel widely; her 'journey' is essentially the Sokoto-Degel-Sifawa-Gwandu axis traced by the Fulani jihad of 1804-1808 and by her later domestic life as caliph's daughter, sister and counsellor. Reconstructed from her own poem Wakar Gewaye ('The Journey'), Jean Boyd's The Caliph's Sister, and Boyd & Mack's Collected Works. Coordinates for Degel and Sifawa are approximate as the original villages no longer have a precise modern fix.

sources: Nana Asma'u, Wakar Gewaye (c. 1839, in Collected Works)
Jean Boyd, The Caliph's Sister: Nana Asma'u 1793-1865, Teacher, Poet and Islamic Leader (1989)
Jean Boyd and Beverly Mack, Collected Works of Nana Asma'u, Daughter of Usman 'dan Fodiyo (1997)
Beverly Mack and Jean Boyd, One Woman's Jihad: Nana Asma'u, Scholar and Scribe (2000)

journey.event: Born in the village of Degel in Gobir to Shehu Usman dan Fodio and his wife Maimuna; named after Asma bint Abi Bakr.
Joined the hijra of the Shehu's community from Degel to Gudu when she was eleven, the founding event of the Sokoto jihad.
Followed the Shehu's army during the years of warfare against Gobir, an experience she later recorded in Wakar Gewaye, 'The Song of Wandering'.
Was at her father's side at age fifteen when the Gobir capital of Alkalawa fell to Bello's forces, the decisive victory of the jihad.
Settled with the Shehu's community at Sifawa, where she married Gidado dan Laima and began her literary career.
Moved to the new capital of Sokoto, founded by her brother Muhammad Bello, where she lived for the rest of her life.
Was present at the death of her father, the Shehu Usman dan Fodio, in Sokoto.
Established the Yan Taru network of itinerant women teachers (jajis), based in Sokoto but reaching outlying villages across the Caliphate.
Was at her brother Muhammad Bello's deathbed; began collaborating with her husband Gidado on chronicles of the Caliphate's founders.
Died in Sokoto, having outlived nearly all the founding generation of the Caliphate; left over sixty surviving works in Arabic, Fulfulde and Hausa.
